input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot with six axes, focusing on the first axis for precise motion control and dynamics analysis in manufacturing environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including real-time monitoring of operational parameters, continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction, dynamic simulation for predictive maintenance, and interactive visualization via a dashboard interface, serving manufacturing engineers and maintenance teams.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for multi-level abstraction modeling. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are used to define component structures, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavior modeling is handled via MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, with detailed simulation performed using MathWorks Simulink and other specialized simulation tools, while general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java support detailed behavioral implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) utilizes SysML for requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer modeling. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral aspects are modeled using MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines, MathWorks Simulink, and general-purpose code (C++, Java). Data sources consist of contextual data traces from physical objects, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes, with heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives integrated across multiple modeling tools.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: One-way automated data flow from physical assets to digital shadow, involving contextual data traces aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes (e.g., continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction in industrial robots), implemented via Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for live-data linking. Specific data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, and data integration mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to industry standards including SysML [17] for model-based systems engineering, AADL [24] for architecture description, ACME [23] for component-based modeling, MontiArc [25], [26] for architectural descriptions, and SysML IBDs [17], [27] for interface and behavior modeling. These standards ensure structured representation of system components, interfaces, and behaviors across abstraction levels, facilitating interoperability among heterogeneous modeling tools and stakeholders.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot with six axes and the focus on the first axis for motion control and friction analysis. It is specific and relevant, but could include more detail about the components (motor, gearboxes) and the explicit purpose of friction understanding. Supported by quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and 'The first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow.'

The extraction accurately describes services like real-time monitoring, continuous friction identification, and dashboard visualization, which are supported by the source. However, 'predictive maintenance' and specific user roles are not explicitly mentioned. The paper discusses a digital shadow (one-way flow) but notes it as a foundation for digital twins. Supported by quotes: 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and 'visualization in a dashboard.'

The extraction is highly specific and directly grounded in the source text, which lists MBSE methodologies, ADLs, behavior modeling tools, simulation tools, and programming languages. Quotes: 'e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT' (Section II.A), 'e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs' (Section II.A), 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML' (Section II.A), 'MathWorks Simulink' (Section II.A), 'general-purpose languages like C++ or Java' (Section II.A).

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source text does not mention any temporal aspects of digital twinning, such as synchronization frequencies, latency, or time scales. The focus is on creating a digital shadow for friction models, with no reference to twinning time scales.

The source text describes a single digital shadow for a specific robot axis, with no mention of multiple instances, hierarchies, or coordination between twins. It focuses on a single instance for friction modeling.

No evidence in the source text regarding digital twin constellations, architecture patterns, or system-wide coordination. The text discusses a specific digital shadow implementation without broader architectural details.

The source text does not discuss integration with external systems, data exchange protocols, or interoperability with other digital twins or enterprise systems. It focuses on internal data and model integration for a specific robot axis.
